source: Invest Like the Best — Tech and consumer growth investing (EP.149)
summary: Video game monetization will converge upward toward cable TV levels ($0.85-$0.90/hr) and potentially to passionate sports fan levels ($2-3/hr), from current $0.08-0.50/hr.
summary_extended: Baker argues that video game monetization is dramatically underpriced relative to the engagement it delivers, and that convergence upward toward cable TV and sports-level monetization is inevitable. He presents specific per-hour figures: mobile games monetize at $0.08–$0.10/hr, console/PC games at $0.50/hr, average cable channels at $0.85–$0.90/hr, and highly engaging content like passionate sports at $2–$3/hr. His reasoning is that consumer engagement in gaming exceeds most TV content, and markets historically price engagement, so monetization must converge upward over time. He also notes that eSports monetization is not yet captured in these figures and represents additional structural upside beyond the base convergence thesis. The investment implication is a long-term bullish view on video game publishers and platforms that can capture this monetization uplift, though Baker does not name specific tickers in this segment.
detail: Mobile games monetize at $0.08-0.10/hr; console/PC games at $0.50/hr; average cable channel at $0.85-0.90/hr; highly engaging content at $2-3/hr. Given engagement levels in gaming exceed most TV content, monetization should converge upward. eSports monetization is not yet included in these figures and represents additional upside.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents specific per-hour monetization figures and frames convergence as 'inevitable.'
quote_fragment: inevitable that monetization is going to increase
